response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between your .NET 7 cross-platform architecture and the WPF libraries, which are strictly tied to the Windows-only .NET Framework runtime. When your application attempts to load the WPF project on Windows, it is likely referencing older assembly versions or expecting the full .NET Framework environment, whereas your main application is built on the modern .NET Core or .NET 5+ model that does not natively include these legacy dependencies in the same way.

To resolve this issue without downgrading your main application to .NET Framework, you must restructure your project to use .NET 7's native WPF support by creating a dedicated WPF project that targets the Windows platform specifically within your solution. This involves setting the `TargetFramework` in your WPF project file to `net7.0-windows` instead of the older `net48` or `net472`, ensuring that the build process correctly resolves the `PresentationFramework` assembly against the .NET 7 runtime rather than the legacy .NET Framework.

cousin projects in your solution that target different frameworks can coexist, provided you manage the conditional compilation and project references correctly to prevent the Mac build from attempting to include Windows-specific UI code. You should utilize conditional compilation symbols or MSBuild properties in your main application to determine the operating system at build time, thereby excluding the WPF project entirely from the Mac build configuration while including it only when targeting Windows.

Implementing an abstraction layer or interface in your shared codebase is essential to decouple your business logic from the specific UI framework, allowing you to inject either the WPF window implementation or the Cocoa window implementation based on the detected platform. This pattern ensures that your core logic remains portable and that the specific UI dependencies are only loaded when the corresponding runtime environment is present, effectively bypassing the missing assembly error on non-Windows platforms.

Finally, verify that your WPF project's `.csproj` file includes the necessary package references for .NET 7 WPF, such as `Microsoft.NET.Sdk`, and ensure that the `UseWPF` property is explicitly set to `true` to trigger the correct SDK features. By aligning your WPF project with the modern .NET 7 tooling and isolating it through conditional build configurations, you can maintain a single codebase that compiles to a native WPF application on Windows and a native Cocoa application on Mac without triggering assembly loading errors.
